title: transaction — field classification
description: Transactions carry no PII directly — subjects are referenced via (reference_type, reference_id) chains to source entities (Invoice / Payment / Refund / Chargeback / CouponRedemption). Amounts are confidential (competitor tenant cross-inference risk). The ledger entries are the audit-critical row-level record. No card data (day-pass modeling's payment_method is a category on facility side; payment tokens live in the payment module — not here).

confidential: Transaction.amount_cents (competitor tenant cross-inference risk — bucketed on egress + never carried in provider webhook payloads)
TransactionLedgerEntry.debit_cents (per-account balance leaks; only egressed to admin + finance role)
restricted: Transaction.metadata (bounded to 8KB but may contain support-context that references PII by ID; treated as restricted for safety — support role sees redacted view where reference chains are not walked)
regulated_financial: TransactionLedgerEntry.id + transaction_id + account + debit_cents + credit_cents + description (the load-bearing double-entry audit record)
audit_log: Transaction.status (every transition — retention 7 years; regulator-critical for reversal audit trail)
Transaction.amount_cents (every read audited for cross-tenant support access)
Transaction.metadata (every mutation logged — mutations are refused by observer, so audit rows fire only on the initial insert)
Transaction.source_transaction_id + reversal_reason (every reversal creates a critical audit row)
TransactionLedgerEntry.debit_cents + credit_cents (every entry logged — retention 7 years)
TransactionLedgerEntry.account (every entry logged — regulator needs to see the account touched)

notes: Transaction.metadata is CONSTRAINED to ≤ 8KB at the observer level — verbose support context lives in the audit trail, not on the transaction row. Bounded to keep the row size predictable + protect against a rogue caller filling it with PII.
TransactionLedgerEntry.description is CONSTRAINED to ≤ 256 chars — auto-generated by the TransactionRecorder from the transaction kind + accounts touched. Not user-input.

amount_cents is 'confidential' rather than 'restricted' because it's aggregatable in a well-scoped tenant context (admin + finance role legitimately view + export it). Support role sees redacted view (amount buckets: <$100, $100-$1k, $1k-$10k, >$10k) — never precise amounts.

PCI-DSS scope: NO card / PAN / CVV / expiry fields on any transaction row. Payment tokens live in the payment module. When a payment_intent_id is captured in Transaction.metadata (rare — only for external-reference audit), it's a token reference, never card data.

regulated_financial tagging is broad — the ENTIRE transaction row + every ledger entry is regulated. This drives the 7-year retention (10 for Enterprise) + archive-to-compliance-storage-before-purge pipeline.

The source_transaction_id + reversal_reason pair is treated with special care — it's the regulator's primary audit trail for corrections. Every reversal writes a critical audit row that outlives both the original + the reversal.

exchange_rate_to_base is 'confidential' (rather than internal) because a competitor knowing a tenant's typical exchange-rate provider + timing can infer FX-hedging strategy. Egressed only to admin + finance role.

created_by_user_id is 'internal' — the actor is knowable to the tenant admin but not egressed to third parties. Nullable on system-recorded transactions (invoice_payment fired by RecordInvoicePaidTransactionJob has no human actor).
